issue.title: Explore porting notes and task files to Pug

user-story.as-a: maintainer working across Markdown notes and XML task files
user-story.i-want: to evaluate whether those authoring formats should move to Pug or a Pug-centered source model
user-story.so-that: the repo can use a more uniform, ergonomic authoring shape without giving up clarity, structure, or reviewability

issue.premise:
The repo currently keeps human-authored notes as Markdown documents and structured work items as XML issue files. That split is workable, but it means authoring conventions, tooling, and formatting differ between two adjacent kinds of project documentation. A Pug-centered approach might offer a more concise writing experience and a shared shape for both looser notes and more structured task records.
This should begin as an exploration, not an assumed migration. Markdown remains highly readable in raw form, XML remains explicit and easy to validate structurally, and both are broadly tool-friendly. Any proposal to port either notes or task files to Pug needs to explain what becomes better, what becomes harder, and how diffs, editor ergonomics, formatting, and agent ingestion would change.
With the likely exception of README-style files, the purpose of these notes is not primarily to render them into another presentation layer. The raw files themselves are the product: humans read them directly in editors and terminals, and agents ingest them as source material. That means a Pug proposal should be treated skeptically if it depends on a render step for ordinary use, weakens raw-file readability, or introduces tooling that makes the canonical text less accessible than today's Markdown and XML.

acceptance-criteria.criterion: The repo has a written evaluation of Pug as a source format for both note-like documents and structured task records, including benefits, drawbacks, and non-goals.

The evaluation compares at least three shapes: keep Markdown and XML as-is, use Pug only for one document class, or adopt a Pug-centered source model, while treating render-dependent workflows as a poor fit outside README-like exceptions.
The exploration identifies how metadata, headings, lists, code blocks, and task-specific structured fields would be represented without losing important readability or validation properties.

The work evaluates raw-file readability and agent-ingestion ergonomics as first-class requirements, including whether a maintainer can comfortably read and edit representative files without compiling or previewing them.

The work includes a concrete proposal for how review ergonomics, formatting, and automation would function, including whether `dprint` can format the chosen source shape directly or would need new plugins, custom tooling, or an intentional gap.
The exploration records whether Pug has solid syntax-highlighting and editing support in the editors we actually favor, and treats weak editor support as a meaningful strike against migration.
If Pug looks viable, the task leaves behind a narrow pilot plan for one representative note file and one representative task file before any broad migration is attempted.

review-items.item: Decide whether Pug would be a direct replacement format, an authoring source for canonical raw files, or merely a templating aid for selected structured documents where raw readability matters less.
Compare raw-file readability carefully, since these notes and task files are meant to be read directly by humans and ingested directly by agents.
Check formatter and editor support early, especially whether `dprint` can participate and whether favored editors offer dependable syntax highlighting and editing behavior for Pug.
Identify the smallest realistic pilot so the evaluation can test author experience, diff quality, automation burden, and ingestion ergonomics before touching the broader corpus.
